title: Gettysburg

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Kill me!
jokes.setup: Boring corporate code of conduct being read aloud
jokes.punchline: Employee's dramatic overreaction to mundane corporate training
jokes.explanation: Standard office comedy opener - relatable workplace boredom expressed dramatically

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Employee
dialogue.line: This is the most boring thing I've ever heard in my entire life. I'm literally dying right now. Someone call an ambulance. I can't feel my legs anymore.

jokes.text: Oh! I'm going into labor!
jokes.setup: Continues reading boring corporate policy
jokes.punchline: Pam fakes going into labor to escape meeting
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Pam's practical solution fits her personality

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the baby... the baby's coming!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We need to get to the hospital, now!

jokes.text: I know it's wrong to fake going into labor just to get out of things, but sometimes it's necessary.
jokes.setup: Just faked labor to escape meeting
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact confession to camera about her deception
jokes.explanation: Perfect talking head moment - casual admission of mild fraud with reasonable justification

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm not actually in labor. I just needed to get out of that meeting. Jim's going to kill me when he finds out.

jokes.text: Or should I have corndogs. I mean-- I'm going into labor!
jokes.setup: Supposedly in labor
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by food options mid-fake-labor
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work - pregnant woman's priorities shine through her deception

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the contractions are getting worse!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, we need to get you to the hospital right now.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Did someone order sandwiches?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, you're in labor!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I know, I know, but is there turkey? I really wanted turkey.

jokes.text: Okay, three reasons you are wrong about true blood. Number one-- I'm going into labor.
jokes.setup: Employee tries to make point about TV show
jokes.punchline: Pam interrupts with same fake labor line
jokes.explanation: Running gag variation - shows how the fake labor becomes a universal escape

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Employee
dialogue.line: It's like that episode of The Office where they had to do the fake labor drill.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's like that episode of The Office where they had to do the fake labor drill.

jokes.text: can't keep saying you're going into labor. Everyone knows you're full of it.
jokes.setup: Pam has been repeatedly faking labor
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact callout of the obvious deception
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim - the voice of reason cutting through office nonsense

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, you've cried wolf like five times this week. I'm not even going to look up from my desk anymore.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Okay. I'm really in labor! This is happening.
jokes.setup: Has been crying wolf about labor
jokes.punchline: Actually goes into real labor after everyone stopped believing her
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the setup - classic comedy structure with real stakes

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh no, I think it's happening! This is it!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, you said that three times yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: False labor. Classic misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious this time! I'm having contractions!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam, buddy, we've all heard this before. Just relax, it'll pass.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: You don't understand, this is real! Someone call an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Sure, Pam. We'll call someone when you actually have the baby.

jokes.text: False alarm.
jokes.setup: Big dramatic exit for 'real' labor
jokes.punchline: Casual admission it was another false alarm
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy - two words deflate all the drama and prove Jim right

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I think this is it. This is real labor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, we're going to the hospital!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait... false alarm.

jokes.text: Business is war!
jokes.setup: Lost another client
jokes.punchline: Andy's dramatic overreaction with war metaphor
jokes.explanation: Sets up Andy's war obsession theme for the episode

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We've lost another client? That's it. That's the third one this month. We're not running a business anymore, we're running a casualty ward. I feel like I'm General Custer right now, except instead of Indians, it's clients, and instead of a horse, I'm in a Sebring.

jokes.text: So today I'm turning the inspire-factor up to ten, with a little help from my friend-- America's bloodiest battle.
jokes.setup: Andy wants to inspire his team
jokes.punchline: Plans to use mass death as inspiration
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Andy's tone-deaf leadership style on full display

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to inspire this team. I've been reading about great leaders throughout history, and you know what they all had in common? They faced adversity. Mass death, plagues, wars... I'm thinking we use that as inspiration.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What I'm saying is, we're going to tackle this quarter like we're soldiers in World War I. Except without the dying. Mostly.

jokes.text: The Civil War history industry has conveniently forgotten about the battle of Schrute Farms.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Civil War battles
jokes.punchline: Dwight claims his family farm was site of forgotten battle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - combines his self-importance, family pride, and conspiracy theories

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My family's beet farm was actually the site of a major Civil War battle that history forgot about.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, that's not true.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It is. The Battle of Schrute Farms. Very significant. General Lee himself walked those fields.

jokes.text: What's the halal option?" "Dates, tabbouleh, and a bagel with cream cheese." "Out."
jokes.setup: Andy offers halal lunch option
jokes.punchline: Stanley immediately rejects the Middle Eastern food combo
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Stanley's blunt food preferences

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Stanley, I ordered some halal for lunch today. There's a great new spot nearby.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: No. Absolutely not.

jokes.text: You know, it's the same as the Kosher option. There's a lesson in there.
jokes.setup: Halal and kosher options are identical
jokes.punchline: Andy pretends this mundane coincidence is profound
jokes.explanation: Andy's fake wisdom - trying to sound deep about lunch logistics

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. The halal and kosher options are exactly the same?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's beautiful, man. Two different religions, two different cultures, and they both arrive at the same answer. It's like... it's like we're all more alike than we think.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's profound.

jokes.text: You're not my slaves. Thanks to Gettysburg.
jokes.setup: Andy can't force people to go on trip
jokes.punchline: References Civil War slavery in casual workplace context
jokes.explanation: Perfectly inappropriate Andy moment - historical reference in wrong context

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I can't force people to go on this trip. That would be like slavery.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Slavery? Jim, fact: slavery was way worse than this.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know that, Dwight. I'm just saying—

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, you don't know. You probably think slavery ended in like 1920.

jokes.text: Guess I'm a sucker for historical fiction.
jokes.setup: Going to historical battlefield
jokes.punchline: Oscar calls actual history 'historical fiction' as dig at Andy
jokes.explanation: Excellent Oscar snark - intellectually superior putdown disguised as agreement

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Wait, you're going to a historical battlefield? That's cool. I love historical fiction.

jokes.text: And-- Oh. You missed your head. There you go.
jokes.setup: Andy handing out Civil War hats
jokes.punchline: Hat placement goes wrong, needs correction
jokes.explanation: Minor physical comedy moment - basic visual gag

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone, I got Civil War hats for everybody!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Now, this one goes on your head... no, not like that. Here, let me adjust it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I know how to wear a Civil War hat, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Clearly you don't, because you've got it on backwards.

jokes.text: a little foreplay before we do it.
jokes.setup: About to watch Civil War documentary
jokes.punchline: Andy uses sexual metaphor inappropriately for workplace context
jokes.explanation: Typical Andy inappropriateness - sexual reference where it doesn't belong

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, settle in. This Civil War documentary is going to blow your minds.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna slip into it real easy, nice and slow, really let it penetrate deep into your consciousness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: By the end, you're all gonna be thoroughly satisfied and exhausted.

jokes.text: Ooh. Isn't the the one where the guy becomes Limitless?
jokes.setup: Discussing the movie Limitless
jokes.punchline: Kevin's circular description of the movie's title as its plot
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kevin logic - tautological movie description shows his thinking

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Limitless is about this guy who takes a drug and it has no limits. That's why it's called Limitless.

jokes.text: Limitless! Limitless! Limitless! Limitless!
jokes.setup: Arguing about watching Civil War documentary vs movie
jokes.punchline: Group chanting movie title like sports fans
jokes.explanation: Group dynamic comedy - office workers rebelling against Andy's plans

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Come on, we should watch the documentary. It's educational.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: No way, the movie is way better.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, the documentary is kind of boring.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: As Assistant Regional Manager, I decide we watch the documentary.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm in charge. And I say... movie!

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: Movie! Movie! Movie! Movie!

jokes.text: Robert, you got your sheep and you got your black sheep, and I'm not even a sheep. I'm on the freaking moon.
jokes.setup: Robert talking about free-thinkers
jokes.punchline: Creed's escalating metaphor about his uniqueness
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed - perfectly bizarre logic delivered with complete confidence

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Free-thinkers. I like that.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm a free-thinker. I think freely. I'm like a bird.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: A beautiful bird that flies above all the other birds.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: In a place that no other bird can reach.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: A place I invented.

jokes.text: Fun fact-- In France, they call Limitless "The Man With Many Capabilities."
jokes.setup: Andy trying to seem knowledgeable
jokes.punchline: Made-up foreign movie title that sounds ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - fake expertise with absurd literal translation

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I've seen that film. It's a classic. 'Das Blinkenschnitzel.' Incredible cinematography.

jokes.text: Unless you're going on the very specially-created and meticulously-researched Andy Bernard Tour.
jokes.setup: Official tour vs Andy's improvised one
jokes.punchline: Andy's grandiose description of his amateur tour
jokes.explanation: Andy's self-importance on display - inflating his amateur efforts

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Dunder Mifflin Scranton branch. We have a real official tour guide, but I thought I'd give you guys my own personal tour instead.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is the bullpen. These are our salespeople. They sell paper. It's very dangerous work.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And over here is the annex. It's basically a dungeon. We keep our worst employees down there.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is the kitchen. We have a refrigerator. Sometimes people eat other people's food. It's a real problem.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And this is my office. Well, it's not really my office yet, but when I'm manager, it will be.

jokes.text: They stopped in this field for a picnic, which they called lunch.
jokes.setup: Andy giving historical tour
jokes.punchline: Mundane 'historical fact' about synonym for meal
jokes.explanation: Perfect mockery of tour guide trivia - completely useless 'fact' delivered seriously

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And this is where we had the historic lunch meeting of 1987.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It was a very important repast.

jokes.text: it makes the battle of Gettysburg sound like a bunch of schoolgirls wrestling over a hairbrush.
jokes.setup: Dwight promoting Schrute Farms battle importance
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous comparison trivializing actual major battle
jokes.explanation: Great Dwight delusion - vivid, absurd imagery to support his false history

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The Battle of Schrute Farms was one of the most pivotal moments in American history. More important than Gettysburg, more significant than D-Day.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, that was a paintball game.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A paintball game that changed the course of this office forever.

jokes.text: Really? Why don't you tell us the real history, Gore Vidal.
jokes.setup: Dwight claiming to know real history
jokes.punchline: Oscar's sarcastic comparison to famous historian
jokes.explanation: Good Oscar intellectualism - literary reference as putdown

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're like a combination of a historian and a... wait, no, you're just wrong.

jokes.text: I don't know who that is, but I'm gonna to tell you this--
jokes.setup: Oscar's Gore Vidal reference
jokes.punchline: Dwight admits ignorance but continues with confidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - admits ignorance but maintains authority anyway

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know who Gore Vidal is, but I'm going to continue talking about him with complete confidence.

jokes.text: Raw fish-- The disgusting food from Japan that Americans would never want to eat. Now we can't get enough of it.
jokes.setup: Kevin pitching business ideas to Robert
jokes.punchline: Describes sushi as if it's breaking news from 1980
jokes.explanation: Kevin's delayed cultural awareness - treating old trend as revolutionary

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I've got a business idea. We open a sushi restaurant, but get this - we tell people it's raw fish. Just raw fish on rice. And we charge them thirty dollars a plate.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: That's... that's already a thing, Kevin.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Not the way I'm gonna do it. I'm gonna make it sound like breaking news. 'We interrupt this regularly scheduled meal to bring you this special report: Fish. Raw. On rice. Back to your dinner.'

jokes.text: Origami. It's the Sushi of paper.
jokes.setup: Kevin's business pitch about Japanese trends
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical comparison between food and paper folding
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Kevin logic - completely meaningless comparison delivered with confidence

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: So I've been looking at Japanese trends, right? And I'm thinking we should start a business where we fold paper into food shapes.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: It's like origami, but you eat it. Or you fold it. I haven't worked out the details yet.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: The point is, Japan's doing great, food's doing great, so obviously this is the next big thing.

jokes.text: "Papyr." Paper for women. It's pink, scented, and silky soft.
jokes.setup: Pitching business innovation
jokes.punchline: Sexist product idea based on outdated gender stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Classic Packer awfulness - perfectly in character but predictably offensive

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: I've got a business innovation that's going to blow your minds. A cleaning product... exclusively for women.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Todd, that's... that's not really an innovation.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: No, hear me out. It smells like... shoes and broken dreams.

jokes.text: The situation you described, returning home to a wife complaining about her paper being too masculine, is not one I'm familiar with.
jokes.setup: Packer's absurd gendered paper pitch
jokes.punchline: Robert's diplomatic way of saying the premise is insane
jokes.explanation: Excellent Robert moment - polite but devastating dismissal of ridiculous idea

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: So you want to sell paper based on gender?

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: I appreciate the creativity, but that's not going to work.

jokes.text: In the African-American community-- No.
jokes.setup: Darryl starts to make cultural pitch
jokes.punchline: Robert immediately shuts it down
jokes.explanation: Good uncomfortable moment - Robert preventing potentially offensive stereotyping

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I was thinking we could do a cultural diversity initiative, you know, really connect with the urban demographic

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Apparently, I bear a passing resemblance to Abraham Lincoln. Makes it kind of hard for me to go to places like museums, historical monuments, elementary schools.
jokes.setup: Man dressed as Lincoln at Gettysburg
jokes.punchline: Complains about resemblance while literally impersonating Lincoln
jokes.explanation: Good irony - professional impersonator complaining about being recognized

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: People keep saying I look just like Abraham Lincoln. I mean, I'm literally dressed as Abraham Lincoln right now, so yeah, I wonder why that is.

jokes.text: I don't see it.
jokes.setup: Lincoln impersonator in full costume and makeup
jokes.punchline: Kevin can't see the obvious resemblance
jokes.explanation: Good Kevin obliviousness - missing the completely obvious

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I don't see it. I mean, the guy's got the beard, the hat, the whole outfit, but I just don't see Lincoln in him.

jokes.text: Some people call me the Great Emancipator, but, uh, you might know me from the penny.
jokes.setup: Lincoln introduction
jokes.punchline: References modern currency instead of historical achievements
jokes.explanation: Decent modern reference joke - Lincoln as celebrity from currency

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: Four score and seven years ago, our forefathers brought forth on this continent a new nation. And you know what? I'd trade it all for a crisp hundred dollar bill right now.

jokes.text: At this point, when you're this pregnant, it's kind of like senior spring. The other day I spit my gum out on the carpet.
jokes.setup: Pam very pregnant and unmotivated
jokes.punchline: Compares pregnancy to senioritis, admits to slovenly behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent analogy and confession - relatable pregnancy experience with specific gross detail

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm like a senior in high school right now. I have senioritis, but for pregnancy.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't care about anything. I'm not doing my hair, I'm not doing my makeup. I'm just wearing sweatpants.

jokes.text: Well, you know how in the vending machine they have the chocolate chip cookies in the A1 spot? They do that 'cause they think a-1's the best spot for the best cookie. But the real best spot is D4.
jokes.setup: Kevin explaining vending machine strategy
jokes.punchline: Detailed analysis of optimal product placement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kevin expertise - deep knowledge of snack food logistics

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: The key to the vending machine is knowing which products are going to shift. You gotta watch the patterns. The chips on the left side, they move faster because people see them first. But the real money is in the bottom rows where nobody looks. That's where you stock the expensive stuff nobody wants anyway. And the sodas? Always keep them cold, always keep them stocked. It's not rocket science, it's vending machine science.

jokes.text: Okay, we are now on a planet where Kevin is the most creative person around, and I am just some good-looking guy.
jokes.setup: Kevin's ideas being praised by Robert
jokes.punchline: Jim's talking head about the absurd reversal of office dynamics
jokes.explanation: Great Jim reaction - perfectly captures the surreal workplace dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So Kevin comes up with this idea, and suddenly Robert's treating him like he's the next Steve Jobs. I mean, the guy once microwaved a whole rotisserie chicken in his pants. And now he's a genius. I don't know what's happening anymore.

jokes.text: Oh, perhaps a trip to the theater will enliven my spirits. No!
jokes.setup: Lincoln impersonator being sad
jokes.punchline: References Ford's Theatre assassination with dramatic timing
jokes.explanation: Good historical irony - Lincoln character almost making fatal mistake

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: I'm having a really tough time. Nothing's going right for me.

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: You know, I'd give anything to go see a play at Ford's Theatre right about now.

jokes.text: 'Kay, so another thing about oatmeal cookies-- Who even wants them? I mean, I've seen Toby eat one, like, once, but other than that... Like, forget it.
jokes.setup: Kevin's product placement theory
jokes.punchline: Uses Toby as the only person desperate enough to eat oatmeal cookies
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Kevin using Toby as example of lowest standards

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: See, product placement is all about finding the right demographic. You need someone so desperate, so willing to eat anything, that they'll consume your product without question.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: That's why I'm using Toby as my test subject for these oatmeal cookies.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: What? Why me?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Because Toby, you're the only person in this office desperate enough to eat them.

jokes.text: How about that two-hole-punch letter? Only the lawyers want that punch at the top, and they use legal.
jokes.setup: Looking for business equivalent of unwanted oatmeal cookies
jokes.punchline: Kevin identifies specific unpopular office product with detailed knowledge
jokes.explanation: Kevin continuing to show unexpected business insight

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You know what? Those are like the oatmeal raisin cookies of office supplies. Nobody wants them, but they keep showing up.

jokes.text: Yeah, that's what I had for breakfast, and I think that's probably why I'm still hungry.
jokes.setup: Andy mentions 'food for thought'
jokes.punchline: Darryl takes it literally as inadequate breakfast
jokes.explanation: Good literal interpretation of metaphor - practical Darryl response

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's food for thought.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: That's not gonna fill me up.

jokes.text: Hunger for hamburger. Hunger for chicken chimichangas. Right, Darryl?
jokes.setup: Andy building dramatic speech about hunger
jokes.punchline: Devolves from metaphorical to literal food cravings
jokes.explanation: Andy's speech deteriorating into actual food discussion

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, hunger is like a... a metaphor for the human condition. We're all searching for something to fill the void inside us.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We hunger for love, for connection, for meaning in this cold and—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Is anyone else thinking about sandwiches right now? Like, a really good sandwich?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm talking deli meat, fresh bread, maybe some of those little pickles on the side...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: God, I could really go for a sandwich.

jokes.text: I commissioned this flag for Dunder Mifflin. Cost me $200. Only $200?
jokes.setup: Andy proud of expensive custom flag
jokes.punchline: Jim's surprise that it cost so little for such quality
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Andy's pride - Jim focusing on wrong aspect

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I had a custom flag made. Cost me $3,000.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Three thousand dollars? For a flag?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's a premium flag, Jim. Hand-stitched, Italian silk...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, you paid three grand and you're surprised it's good quality? I thought you were gonna say it fell apart.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, no. I'm very happy with it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you spent three thousand dollars on a flag and you're not even mad? That's the most Andy thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: Andy, this in inappropriate. People died here, man.
jokes.setup: Andy staging mock Civil War battle at Gettysburg
jokes.punchline: Oscar's serious objection to the disrespectful behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Oscar moment - being the voice of propriety and respect

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: This is deeply offensive. The Civil War was the deadliest conflict in American history. 620,000 people died. And you're out here playing dress-up like it's a theme party.

jokes.text: You know, all I had for breakfast was oatmeal, yoghurt, um, coffee, orange juice, and toast. Two poached eggs, and then that half a sandwich on the bus.
jokes.setup: Phyllis claiming she can't walk because she didn't eat enough
jokes.punchline: Lists increasingly large breakfast that contradicts her claim
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Phyllis undermining her own excuse with excessive detail

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I can't walk, I didn't eat enough breakfast.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I had two eggs, bacon, toast, hash browns, and a glass of orange juice.

jokes.text: Said you weren't gonna look back.
jokes.setup: Andy dramatically declares he won't look back
jokes.punchline: Jim calls him out for immediately looking back
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim - catching Andy in his dramatic contradiction immediately

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not looking back. Not once. I'm done.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You just looked back.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You totally did. You looked back like three times.

jokes.text: You have to understand, poets, artists, dancers, these kinds of men preferred peace to war. These delicate, lovely men found a place of refuge among the Schrutes at Schrute Farm.
jokes.setup: Dwight expecting story of battle glory at Schrute Farms
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was actually a refuge for sensitive artistic men
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff - completely undermines Dwight's masculine battle fantasy with opposite reality

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Tell me about the glorious battles fought at Schrute Farms.

dialogue.speaker: Archivist
dialogue.line: Well, Dwight, Schrute Farms was actually a refuge for sensitive artistic men.

jokes.text: I like to think of Schrute Farm as, uh, the underground railroad for the sensitive and, well, fabulous.
jokes.setup: Continuing Schrute Farm revelation
jokes.punchline: Compares it to Underground Railroad but for artistic men
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper - historical parallel with delicate word choice destroys Dwight's narrative

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The Schrute Farm is like an Underground Railroad, but for artistic men.

jokes.text: Never trust a cookie with a woman's name. Pecan Sandie. Lorna Doone. Madeleine. Oh, they'll just break your heart.
jokes.setup: Kevin's cookie theories continue
jokes.punchline: Applies relationship advice to baked goods with specific examples
jokes.explanation: Excellent Kevin moment - perfectly absurd logic with genuine emotional delivery

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You know, relationships are a lot like cookies. You gotta know when to pull them out before they get too hard.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: And sometimes you gotta add a little extra sweetness to keep things fresh. That's why I always use brown sugar instead of white.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: The problem with most people's relationships is they don't let them cool down after you take them out of the oven. You gotta be patient.

jokes.text: Every time you buy a Big Mac, yet set one ingredient aside. Then, at the end of the week, you have a free Big Mac. And you love it even more, because you made it with your own hands.
jokes.setup: Ryan pitching 'Big Mac Idea' to Robert
jokes.punchline: Completely impractical food storage scheme presented as business innovation
jokes.explanation: Good Ryan delusion - treating terrible idea as brilliant with no awareness of flaws

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: So here's my idea: we revolutionize food storage by stacking Big Macs horizontally instead of vertically. It's all about maximizing shelf space.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Ryan, that's... that's just how they already come in the box.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm not changing anything. That's the innovation.

jokes.text: It was just actually cookies the whole time.
jokes.setup: Extended business meeting with various ideas
jokes.punchline: Kevin realizes his cookie obsession dominated the discussion
jokes.explanation: Nice Kevin self-awareness moment - recognizing his fixation

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, how many times did I mention cookies in that meeting?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Fourteen.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: That was about the budget proposal!

jokes.text: Our office has a disease, and it goes by many names-- Sarcasm, snark, wisecracks. You take things that people care about, and you make them feel lame about it with your jokes.
jokes.setup: Andy confronting Jim about trip attitude
jokes.punchline: Calls out the entire show's comedic premise as a disease
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta moment - character criticizing the show's own comedic style

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what, Jim? I've been thinking about your attitude on this trip, and you know what I realized?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This whole office — this is a disease. We're all just... we're all just documentarians of our own mediocrity. That's the joke. That's the whole thing.

jokes.text: I'm pretty sure "DM does GB" means something kind of sexual.
jokes.setup: Jim wearing Andy's custom hat all day
jokes.punchline: Realizes the acronym has unintended sexual interpretation
jokes.explanation: Good awkward realization - innocent design with unfortunate implications

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, what does this hat even stand for?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's my initials, man. A.N.D.Y.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A.N.D.Y... Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Andy, I've been wearing this all day. Do you realize what this acronym looks like?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's... it's not appropriate for the workplace.

jokes.text: I need her like I need a hole in the head.
jokes.setup: Lincoln complaining about Mary Todd
jokes.punchline: Uses phrase about head wound when he's about to be shot in the head
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark irony - perfectly timed idiom before assassination

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: Mary Todd has been driving me crazy. She won't stop nagging me about the war, the economy, everything!

dialogue.speaker: Abraham Lincoln Impersonator
dialogue.line: I swear, if one more thing goes wrong today, I'm going to lose my head.

tmdb_overview: Andy takes some of the office staff on a field trip to Gettysburg for inspiration. Meanwhile, the rest of the office, under orders from Robert California, are tasked with coming up with a new business idea for Dunder Mifflin. Kevin proposes several ideas that catch California's attention, although they are rather idiotic.
insight: Field trip concept struggles: 74-score episode relies on character comedy when plot momentum stalls.
